---
title: "sgsss_decomp"
subtitle: "Workshop [DATE]"
author: "Ben Matthews & Josiah King"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
file.copy("dgp_preprint.pdf","docs/dgp_preprint.pdf")
```

# Overview


# Schedule

|         |             |
| ------- | ----------- |
| ?? - ?? | coffee |
| ?? - ?? | [BM: Introduction to standardisation and decomposition](lecture00.html) |
| ?? - ?? | [JK: practical 2, 3 factor by hand](lecture01.html) |
| ?? - ?? | [BM: bivariate/multivariate](lecture02.html) |
| ?? - ?? | lunch |
| ?? - ?? | [JK: crossclassified](lecture03.html) |
| ?? - ?? | [BM: uncertainty](lecture04.html) |
| ?? - ?? | coffee |
| ?? - ?? | [BM: category effects](lecture05.html) |
| ?? - ?? | [JK: other methods of decomposition](lecture06.html) |




- in terms of the calculations we're going to show them, the idea of having sub-population rates only really fits into the picture when we introduce cross classified stuff? otherwise it just causes more of an issue by having to have a sum at the start of everything. 
    - it's quite hard to come up with a rate that is the product of 2 or 3 factors that **isn't** sub-populations. DG tends to use birth rate related things, because they essentially go down through a series: women>sexuallyactive>pregnant>birthed. could we use something (maybe SCJS) like victimofcrime>victimofpropertycrime>victimofburglary ? 


- somewhere we should talk about the unit of analysis being populations. 
    - i'm not quite sure what i mean by that really. 
    - consider 2 pops: uk, italy. death rate from covid is higher in italy because higher proportion of over 70s.  
    - all of this std/decomp stuff **doesn't** allow you to say:  
    - if an italian was british, the prob of them dying would be XX.    
    - instead it is:  
    - if italy was like britain, the rate of deaths would be XX  
    - maybe that's obvious though. it feels more blurred when it's in the oaxaca approach for some reason.
